findings.problem: The chapter assigns the fresh-contamination odor profile to residue that Aldric identifies as residual. Maren reads the road residue near the well as ozone-sweet, and the three-month Wollen contamination carries the same ozone-sweet cue even after Aldric labels it residual grade three. The continuity sheet establishes ozone-sweet as fresh active contamination and older residue as drier or sourer, so this breaks a canonical sensory rule the book uses to make contamination legible.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise the smell language at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:129 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:155 so aged residue reads as old contamination rather than fresh ozone bleed. If you want Maren to catch mixed notes, make the text distinguish any fresh trace from the settled residual field before Aldric's grade call at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:157.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if a cold read of the revised passages at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:129 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:155 can no longer honestly describe residual contamination as ozone-sweet without qualification, and the sensory description matches Aldric's residual diagnosis at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:157. Fail if old residue still smells like fresh active contamination on the page.

findings.problem: The Wollen visit is the chapter's proof-of-harm scene, but the text never cleanly states why Maren is at this house today or what Britt thinks this visit is for. Britt's 'How does it look?' implies a concrete purpose, yet the scene enters without naming the appointment logic, so the setup initially reads like an author-arranged example rather than the next believable task in Maren's workday.
findings.rewrite_direction: In the scene entry around snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:149, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:151, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:153, add one explicit sentence establishing the trigger for today's visit and Britt's expectation: a scheduled follow-up, worsening animal symptoms, Maren making reassurance rounds, or another equally concrete cause. Keep the scene's existing moral payload, but let the reader know why this doorstep conversation is happening before Maren lies at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:159.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, before Aldric's diagnosis at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:157 or Maren's reassurance at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:159, a cold reader can answer both 'Why did Maren come to the Wollen house today?' and 'What does Britt expect him to check or confirm?' Fail if those answers still have to be reverse-engineered after the scene is already underway.

findings.problem: The chapter's tracked setup for Maren's unwritability is supposed to land lightly, but Aldric nearly states the later reveal outright. He invokes rare Order records, says the System appears unable to reach Maren, and uses the same 'write on you' logic that later functions as a major recontextualizing payoff. That turns a seed into an early partial answer and weakens the long-range mystery architecture.
findings.rewrite_direction: Pull back the explicit reveal language at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:53, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:59, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:69. Aldric can stay unsettlingly specific about anomaly, nonstandard blindness, and professional interest, but he should not yet hand the reader the clean chapter-12 paraphrase that the System cannot write on Maren or that the condition is already half-classified in Order terms.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised opening still makes Aldric sound unusually informed, but a first-time reader cannot honestly summarize the beat before snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:71 as 'Maren is unwritable and Aldric basically knows it already.' Fail if the scene still gives the later payoff away in near-payoff language instead of merely planting suspicion.

findings.problem: The opening keeps reaching for the same 'as if' explanatory scaffold when describing Maren's absence from the System. Because the deprivation beat, the blank-profile beat, the architecture-refusal beat, and the consciousness-anomaly beat all arrive in that same construction, the sentences start to feel mechanically generated rather than escalated by new pressure.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework at least two of the explanatory beats around snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:13, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:47, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:53, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:59 so they do not all hinge on 'as if' analogy. Keep the strongest single comparison if you want it, but let the other beats land through direct description, a different image shape, or Aldric's report syntax.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised opening no longer stacks multiple System-absence beats in the same 'as if' construction across snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:13 through snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:59, while still preserving the information that Maren's public profile exists and the underlying architecture is wrong. Fail if the passage still repeats the same comparison template often enough to read as autopilot.

summary: Award fails by default here: the Maren/Aldric dynamic is lively and the dialogue stays character-true, but the chapter is not yet shortlist-level. It contains a hard continuity break in contamination scent, enters the Wollen proof-of-harm scene without enough causal setup, overstates a tracked later reveal instead of planting it lightly, and leans on a repeated 'as if' template hard enough to flatten the opening.
